name: sqlite
context: SQLite embedded database — zero-config, serverless, single-file SQL engine for local-first apps, CLI tools, and agents
info: SQLite is the most widely deployed database engine in the world. It is a self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration, transactional SQL database that stores an entire database in a single file. Unlike client-server databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL), SQLite runs in-process — the database engine is linked directly into the application. This makes it the default choice for local-first applications, CLI tools, mobile apps, embedded systems, and AI agent workflows that need structured data without infrastructure. SQLite supports most of SQL-92, plus modern features like CTEs, window functions, JSON operators, UPSERT, RETURNING clauses, full-text search (FTS5), and R-Tree spatial indexing. Agents use SQLite skills to create schemas, write queries, tune performance with WAL mode and PRAGMAs, and extend functionality with extensions like FTS5 and JSON. See [[sqlite/schema]] for table design, [[sqlite/queries]] for reading and writing data, [[sqlite/wal]] for concurrency via Write-Ahead Logging, [[sqlite/performance]] for tuning PRAGMAs, and [[sqlite/extensions]] for FTS5, JSON, and loadable modules.
body:
# SQLite

SQLite is a C-language library that implements a small, fast, self-contained, high-reliability, full-featured, SQL database engine. It is the most used database engine in the world — every phone, browser, and operating system ships with it.

## Why SQLite

- **Zero configuration** — no server process, no setup, no administration
- **Single file** — the entire database is a single cross-platform file on disk
- **Serverless** — reads and writes directly to ordinary disk files
- **Transactional** — ACID-compliant with full support for transactions, savepoints, and rollback
- **Cross-platform** — the database file format is stable, cross-platform, and backwards compatible
- **Small footprint** — ~600KB library, minimal memory usage

## Core Skills

1. **[[sqlite/schema]]** — Design tables, indexes, views, triggers, and constraints
2. **[[sqlite/queries]]** — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CTEs, window functions, JSON, UPSERT
3. **[[sqlite/wal]]** — Write-Ahead Logging for concurrent readers and writers
4. **[[sqlite/performance]]** — PRAGMA tuning, indexing strategy, EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN
5. **[[sqlite/extensions]]** — FTS5 full-text search, JSON functions, R-Tree, loadable modules

## Quick Start

```bash
# Create or open a database
sqlite3 myapp.db

# Create a table
sqlite3 myapp.db "CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT UNIQUE, created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')));"

# Insert data
sqlite3 myapp.db "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com');"

# Query data
sqlite3 myapp.db "SELECT * FROM users;"
```

## Using SQLite from Code

### Python (built-in)
```python
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect('myapp.db')
conn.execute('PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL')
conn.execute('PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON')

cursor = conn.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?', ('Alice',))
rows = cursor.fetchall()
conn.close()
```

### Node.js (better-sqlite3)
```javascript
import Database from 'better-sqlite3';

const db = new Database('myapp.db');
db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');

const users = db.prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?').all('Alice');
db.close();
```

### Bash (sqlite3 CLI)
```bash
sqlite3 myapp.db <<'SQL'
  .mode json
  SELECT * FROM users;
SQL
```

## Recommended Connection Setup

Always configure these PRAGMAs when opening a connection:

```sql
PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;          -- concurrent readers + writers
PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL;        -- safe with WAL, much faster
PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;           -- enforce referential integrity
PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000;         -- wait 5s on lock instead of failing
PRAGMA cache_size = -64000;         -- 64MB page cache
PRAGMA temp_store = MEMORY;         -- keep temp tables in RAM
```

## File Format

SQLite database files are self-describing, cross-platform, and stable. The file format has been backwards compatible since version 3.0.0 (2004). A database file created on one platform can be freely copied to and used on any other platform.

## When to Use SQLite

| Use Case | SQLite? |
|----------|--------|
| CLI tools and scripts | Yes |
| Mobile/desktop apps | Yes |
| Local-first web apps | Yes |
| Agent data storage | Yes |
| Embedded/IoT devices | Yes |
| Test databases | Yes |
| Medium-traffic websites | Yes |
| High-write concurrency | Consider PostgreSQL |
| Multi-server apps | Consider PostgreSQL |
| Terabyte-scale data | Consider PostgreSQL |

## Environment

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `sqlite3` | CLI shell (ships with SQLite) |
| `better-sqlite3` | Synchronous Node.js driver (fastest) |
| `sqlite3` (Python) | Built-in Python module |
| `@libsql/client` | Turso/libSQL driver |
| `rusqlite` | Rust bindings |

sections.title: Quick start
sections.body: Install is usually unnecessary — `sqlite3` ships with macOS and most Linux distributions. On Debian/Ubuntu: `sudo apt install sqlite3`. On macOS: already installed. Create a database with `sqlite3 myapp.db`. The file is created on first write. Use `.tables`, `.schema`, and `.help` for interactive exploration.

sections.title: Connection setup PRAGMAs
sections.body: Always set `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` for concurrent access (see [[sqlite/wal]]). Set `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL` for WAL mode (safe and fast). Enable `PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON` (off by default). Set `PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000` to retry on locks instead of failing immediately. Increase `PRAGMA cache_size=-64000` for better read performance. See [[sqlite/performance]] for full tuning guide.

sections.title: Language drivers
sections.body: Python: `import sqlite3` (built-in, synchronous). Node.js: `better-sqlite3` (synchronous, fastest), `sql.js` (WASM, browser-compatible), `@libsql/client` (Turso-compatible). Rust: `rusqlite`. Go: `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure Go) or `github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3` (CGo). All drivers support the same SQL and PRAGMA commands.

sections.title: When to use SQLite
sections.body: Use SQLite for: CLI tools, local-first apps, mobile/desktop apps, embedded systems, test databases, prototyping, agent data stores, medium-traffic websites (<100K hits/day), data analysis, and application file formats. Avoid when: you need high-write concurrency from multiple servers, multi-terabyte datasets, or network-accessible database services. SQLite is not a replacement for PostgreSQL — it's a replacement for `fopen()`.

links.context: Design tables, indexes, views, and constraints
Read and write data with SQL queries
Enable concurrent readers and writers with WAL mode
FTS5, JSON, R-Tree, and loadable extensions

related.note: Prisma supports SQLite as a database provider for Node.js/TypeScript ORMs
Drizzle ORM supports SQLite via better-sqlite3, bun:sqlite, and @libsql/client drivers
Python includes sqlite3 as a built-in standard library module
The sqlite3 CLI integrates naturally into shell scripts and pipelines

author: dojo-community
